steps.instruction: Collect white wood ash (hardwood is best).
steps.helpDetail: Do not use ash from trash or plastic fires. Sift out charcoal bits.

steps.instruction: Leach the lye: Pour water over ash in a filtered container.
steps.helpDetail: Let it drip slowly. The resulting brown liquid is potassium hydroxide (lye). Determine strength by floating an egg (should float halfway).

steps.instruction: Mix lye water with animal fat (tallow) or oil.
steps.helpDetail: Heat gently until it thickens (trace). Pour into molds and let cure for weeks.

techniques.name: Three Sisters Planting
techniques.subtitle: Corn, Beans, Squash symbiosis

steps.instruction: Plant Corn first in a mound.
steps.helpDetail: Corn provides the vertical structure for beans to climb.

steps.instruction: Plant Pole Beans around the corn once it's 6 inches tall.
steps.helpDetail: Beans fix nitrogen in the soil to feed the corn.

steps.instruction: Plant Squash/Pumpkin around the base.
steps.helpDetail: Squash leaves shade the soil, retaining moisture and preventing weeds.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

steps.instruction: Select only the best, healthiest plants.
steps.helpDetail: Do not eat these. Let them fully mature and dry on the plant if possible.

steps.instruction: Harvest seeds and dry them completely.
steps.helpDetail: For wet seeds (tomatoes), ferment in water first to remove gel. Dry on paper.

steps.instruction: Store in a cool, dark, dry place (glass jar).
steps.helpDetail: Include desiccant (silica or dry rice). Label with year.

techniques.name: Composting
techniques.subtitle: Turn waste into rich soil for growing food

steps.instruction: Choose a spot away from water and shelter — ideally shaded.
steps.helpDetail: Compost heaps attract insects and smell. Keep at least 20 feet from sleeping area. Direct shade slows drying but won't stop decomposition.

steps.instruction: Layer green (nitrogen) and brown (carbon) materials alternately.
steps.helpDetail: Green: food scraps, fresh grass, fish guts, manure. Brown: dry leaves, bark, wood chips, straw. Aim for 3 parts brown to 1 part green. Add a thin layer of soil between each layer.

steps.instruction: Keep the pile moist like a wrung-out sponge.
steps.helpDetail: Too wet = anaerobic rot (stinks). Too dry = nothing happens. Add water during dry spells. Cover loosely with leaves in rain.

steps.instruction: Turn the pile every 1-2 weeks with a stick or improvised fork.
steps.helpDetail: Turning introduces oxygen and speeds decomposition. A pile that heats up in the center is working correctly. Should feel warm to the touch.

steps.instruction: Compost is ready when it's dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling.
steps.helpDetail: Takes 4-8 weeks in warm conditions. Mix into garden soil. Never compost meat, dairy, or human waste — these attract predators and spread disease.

steps.instruction: Crush charcoal from your fire into a fine powder.
steps.helpDetail: Use charcoal from hardwood. It must be completely cool.

steps.instruction: Mix with a little water (and salt/mint if available).
steps.helpDetail: Charcoal absorbs toxins and cleans teeth abrasively. Do not use daily due to enamel wear.

steps.instruction: Scrub teeth with finger or chew stick.
steps.helpDetail: Rinse thoroughly.

steps.instruction: Scrape all flesh and fat from the hide.
steps.helpDetail: This is crucial. Use a dull blade or stone tool.

steps.instruction: Mash the animal's brain in water to make a slurry.
steps.helpDetail: Every animal has enough brains to tan its own hide. Rub this mixture into the hide thoroughly.

steps.instruction: Stretch and smoke the hide.
steps.helpDetail: Pull it constantly until dry to keep it soft. Smoke it over a fire to waterproof and preserve it.

steps.instruction: Collect flexible vines, willow shoots, or roots.
steps.helpDetail: Soak them in water to make them pliable.

steps.instruction: Create the base (spokes) first.
steps.helpDetail: Cross sticks in a star pattern and weave around them.

steps.instruction: Weave the sides up (over-under pattern).
steps.helpDetail: Keep tension even. Finish the rim securely by tucking ends back in.
